title: We Asked for Butter; Are We Being Given Lime?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The policy figures are translated in the Indian numbering style used by the source. The Tamil reading copy restores source-confirmed figures, including `1,66,187` total teachers and `98,291` women teachers, regularises OCR-damaged list markers, and corrects unambiguous OCR forms such as `உயார்த்தப்பட்டுள்ளது` and `மறுமணத்்`. The repeated reservation and prohibition passages are retained because they are printed more than once in the source. The canonical Tamil source file is unchanged.
paragraphs: On the final day of the Assembly session, the AIADMK government hurriedly introduced a bill raising the reservation for women in Tamil Nadu's local bodies to fifty per cent. Without any advance notice or debate, it was introduced and unanimously passed by the Assembly on the very same day. Even little girls in our villages can understand that the reason for the AIADMK government's sudden concern for women is the Assembly general election fast approaching. For five years, they did not spare even half a moment's thought for this. Now, with the election only two months away, they have made this announcement to capture the attention of mothers and accomplish their purpose. But the mothers of Tamil Nadu know very well that the AIADMK government acts according to the proverb: “Hold their feet until your purpose is achieved; once it is achieved, seize them by the throat!”

Important resolutions of this kind are normally introduced in the Assembly two days in advance, listed beforehand in the next day's order of business, and then debated before a decision is taken. But this resolution was both introduced and debated on the same day. That alone makes it clear that it is an election-time concession from the ruling party.

On the final day of the Assembly session, the AIADMK government hurriedly introduced a bill raising the reservation for women in Tamil Nadu's local bodies to fifty per cent. Without any advance notice or debate, it was introduced and unanimously passed by the Assembly on the very same day. Even little girls in our villages can understand that the reason for the AIADMK government's sudden concern for women is the Assembly general election fast approaching.

But it was under the DMK government in 1996 that mothers were first given 33 per cent reservation in local bodies. As a result, 44,143 women across Tamil Nadu obtained positions in local administration. In 1989, it was under the DMK government that, for the first time in India, 30 per cent reservation for women in government employment was implemented, opening the way for tens of thousands of women to obtain government jobs. In 1990, it was under the DMK government that, for the first time in India, a separate law granting women equal property rights was enacted. Women were given 33 per cent reservation in local bodies. At this moment, I consider it my duty to recall the achievements made for the mothers of Tamil Nadu while the DMK was in office.

- In 1973, women police officers were appointed to the Tamil Nadu Police for the first time. As a result, thousands of women now enjoy employment in the police force, rising even to the rank of Additional Director General of Police.
- In 1975, schemes providing free sewing machines to poor women and supporting widow remarriage were launched.

- To eradicate caste differences, an inter-caste marriage scheme was begun in 1968 and encouraged with a gold medal. In 1989, it was named the Anjugam Ammaiyar Memorial Inter-Caste Marriage Financial Assistance Scheme, and couples entering an inter-caste marriage were given Rs 5,000. In 1996, the assistance was raised to Rs 10,000. Where one spouse was an Adi Dravidar, it was raised to Rs 20,000 from 1997 and to Rs 25,000 from 2010.

- In 1989, the Moovalur Ramamirtham Ammaiyar Memorial Marriage Assistance Scheme was introduced to help with the marriages of poor women who had studied through Class VIII, providing Rs 5,000. To encourage the educational advancement of rural women, the assistance was raised in 1996 to Rs 10,000 for poor women who had studied through Class X; it rose to Rs 15,000 in 2006, Rs 20,000 in 2008 and Rs 25,000 in 2010.

- Under three schemes—the E. V. R. Maniammaiyar Memorial Marriage Assistance Scheme for the daughters of widowed mothers, the Dr Dharmambal Memorial Widow Remarriage Scheme, and the Mother Teresa Memorial Marriage Assistance Scheme for Destitute Women—the assistance was progressively raised from Rs 5,000 in 1989 to Rs 7,000 in 1997–98, Rs 10,000 in 1999–2000, Rs 15,000 in 2007, Rs 20,000 in 2008 and Rs 25,000 in 2010.

- In 1989, the E. V. R. Nagammaiyar Memorial Free Degree Education Scheme for Women was implemented to encourage poor women to pursue higher education. From 2008, the scheme provided poor women free education through postgraduate study.

- In 1989, the Dr Muthulakshmi Reddy Memorial Maternity Assistance Scheme was implemented to provide financial help for poor pregnant women to obtain nutrition. From 2006, pregnant women received Rs 6,000 under this scheme.

- The monthly pension of Rs 100 paid to destitute widows and women deserted by their husbands was raised to Rs 150 in 1997 and Rs 200 in 2000. The Rs 200 pension was then raised to Rs 400 in 2006 and Rs 500 in 2010.

- On 2 June 1998, an order was issued allowing widows and women deserted by their husbands to receive assistance under the old-age assistance scheme without any cap on the number of beneficiaries, even if they had sons over the age of eighteen.

- As a livelihood scheme for women in poverty, a new programme began in July 2008, providing monthly assistance of Rs 400 to 12,904 poor women over fifty who had remained unmarried and lived in circumstances where they could not support themselves through work.
- Time-scale pay and a pension scheme were sanctioned for the benefit of 2,12,981 nutritious-meal workers.
- To ensure that rural women could receive maternity assistance at any time, additional nurses and doctors were appointed at every primary health centre, providing round-the-clock medical care.
- It was made law that one woman must be appointed as a trustee to the boards of trustees administering temples.

- Through the Women's Development Programme launched by the DMK government in Dharmapuri in May 1989, numerous women's self-help groups were formed in rural areas throughout Tamil Nadu. These enabled rural women to come together, undertake businesses, earn income and improve their standard of living.
- As promised in the 2006 election manifesto, the daily allowance paid to women's self-help group members attending training was raised from Rs 7.50 to Rs 45.
- There were 4,74,874 women's self-help groups operating with 73 lakh and 60 thousand women members.

- Bank loans amounting to Rs 9,845 crore and 20 lakh had been extended to these groups from 2009. Of this, Rs 8,080 crore was extended in the four and a half years after May 2006 alone. This is excellent evidence of the concern the DMK government showed for women's advancement through this programme.
- From 2006–07, a revolving-fund scheme for urban self-help groups was introduced. Over five years, 96,699 urban self-help groups received Rs 96.70 crore as revolving-fund grants.

- Ordinarily, only 25,000 to 30,000 self-help groups received revolving funds each year. But in 2008–09, the government decided to extend revolving-fund grants to all 1.50 lakh eligible self-help groups that had not yet received them. Accordingly, all 1,50,000 such groups received Rs 150 crore in revolving funds together with bank loans.
- In 2009–10, approval was given to provide Rs 70 crore in revolving-fund grants to 70,000 self-help groups, and funds were issued to 69,000 groups.

- In 2010–11, Rs 70 crore was sanctioned to provide revolving-fund grants to 50,000 rural and 20,000 urban self-help groups. So far, 63,967 groups have received the funds. Under the previous AIADMK government, the pay scale of nutritious-meal and anganwadi workers was not raised even once. Under the DMK government, it was raised three times.

- It was under the DMK government that nutritious-meal and anganwadi workers received one increment on completing ten years of service and another on completing twenty years. Nutritious-meal cooks and assistants and anganwadi assistants who had worked for consolidated pay under the AIADMK government were all placed on a special pay scale under the DMK government.

Tamil Nadu now has three and a half crore women. They all demanded that TASMAC shops be shut, that families being destroyed by alcohol be saved, and that prohibition be implemented immediately, and they conducted a moral protest against the AIADMK government. The AIADMK government's police humiliated the protesting women, violated their human rights and went so far as to arrest them. All this burns like fire hidden beneath ash in the hearts of Tamil Nadu's women. This fifty per cent reservation is intended to divert their attention from it.

- It was under the DMK government that, for the first time, nutritious-meal and anganwadi workers received a monthly house-rent allowance and city compensatory allowance from 1 June 2009, together with a medical allowance of Rs 100 and a festival advance of Rs 2,000. The AIADMK government paid these workers no monthly pension. Under the DMK government, retired nutritious-meal organisers, anganwadi cooks and first-grade anganwadi assistants, and cooking assistants and second-grade anganwadi assistants received pensions for the first time, at Rs 700, Rs 600 and Rs 500 a month respectively.
- A scheme providing grants of up to Rs 50,000 to 250 first-generation women entrepreneurs was introduced in 1996, and assistance was distributed.
- A scheme giving women entrepreneurs priority in the allotment of industrial plots within industrial estates began in 1996.
- Twenty-five motorised vehicles were given free to fisherwomen's cooperative societies to assist fisherwomen in selling fish.
- Beginning in Madurai in 1998 and later expanding to other areas, fair-price shops run entirely by women were opened at 230 locations and operated successfully.
- Under the women's microcredit scheme implemented through rural women's self-help groups, loans totalling Rs 2.33 crore were extended to 535 women's groups through October 2000.
- The scheme was extended experimentally to rural areas in Tiruvallur and Coimbatore districts. Because of the welcome it received, it was subsequently extended to rural areas in every district.

- Orders issued by the DMK government opened the way for more women to be appointed as teachers in primary schools. Of the 1,66,187 teachers employed in schools under the DMK government, 98,291 were women.

- Under a scheme launched on 1 June 1975, all widows received assistance without any limit on the number of beneficiaries during the DMK government. From 1996, 3,86,149 widows benefited from the scheme. From 1996, 63,566 women deserted by their husbands also benefited.

Tamil Nadu now has three and a half crore women. They all demanded that TASMAC shops be shut, that families being destroyed by alcohol be saved, and that prohibition be implemented immediately, and they conducted a moral protest against the AIADMK government. The AIADMK government's police humiliated the protesting women, violated their human rights and went so far as to arrest them. All this burns like fire hidden beneath ash in the hearts of Tamil Nadu's women. This fifty per cent reservation is intended to divert their attention from it. It may raise the 44,143 women who first obtained positions in local administration under the DMK government to around 60,000—an increase of about 16,000. Instead of coming forward to fulfil the yearning for prohibition in the hearts of three and a half crore women, the AIADMK government tries to deceive them by offering another 16,000 positions. It is like showing a “paper flower” to women who ask for a real flower. The women of Tamil Nadu asked for “butter”; is Jayalalithaa giving them “lime”?
With affection,
M. K.
24 February 2016
